observations.recommendation: 1. the Municipal Mayor: a. ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers to agencies, to prevent the accumulation of unsettled balances; b. in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of dormant unliquidated cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008; c. direct the concerned Officers and Employees still connected with the agency to immediately submit their liquidation papers to settle their unliquidated cash advances; d. consider applying the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring accountable officers and officials by withholding their salaries as cited in Section 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-004 if warranted. 2. the Municipal Accountant: a. consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers with outstanding cash advances and are still employed with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof; and b. refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee with existing unliquidated cash advance. However, if it is necessary to grant cash advances for special activities, Management must grant the same to the designated Special Disbursing Officer.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, prepared documents to support the request for write-off. Demand letters were issued to the accountable officers; however, only those active employees responded. The concerned officers and employees still connected with the agency were directed to immediately submit their liquidation papers and supporting documents. Some collectors/payees were already directed to pay or had salary deductions applied subject to availability of net take-home pay.
observations.reason: Applying withholding of salaries could not be fully implemented due to regulation prohibiting deductions that would reduce an employee’s monthly net take-home pay to less than five thousand pesos; limited responses to demand letters; the LGU is still gathering documents to support a write-off request to COA.

observations.recommendation: 1. The Municipal Assessor, as mandated by law, to prepare an updated schedule of fair market values and undertake a general revision of real property assessments, for the different classes of real property situated in the Municipality. Henceforth, the Municipal Assessor to observe the equitable adjustment of property values as imposed by law. 2. The Local Government Unit thru the Sangguniang Panlalawigan to prioritize the move to enact through ordinance the general revisions of fair market values and assessment levels thereto, of real properties, for local taxation purposes.
observations.managementAction: A public consultation on the 2027 schedule of market values of real properties was conducted. The publication of SMV is on its second phase. The RPVARA shall be implemented prior to general revision. The proposal was endorsed to the Provincial Government Sangguniang Panlalawigan and is scheduled for prioritization. The Municipal Assessor coordinated with BLGF and the Provincial Assessor for preparation of the updated SMV.
observations.reason: The publication of the SMV is ongoing (second phase) and the RPVARA shall be implemented prior to general revision; enactment through ordinance and full implementation are pending completion of the SMV process and prioritization by the Provincial Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the MGSO for the conduct of physical inventory count, updating of inventory reports/records and reconciliation thereof. The Municipal Accountant to expedite substantiation of recorded 'for reconciliation' PPE items based on the result of the inventory count as recommended above, with the aid of other relevant corroborating documents, in accordance with the applicable guidelines; and coordinate with the MGSO for the conduct of physical inventory count, updating of inventory reports/records and reconciliation thereof.
observations.managementAction: A partial physical count was done. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the MGSO for inventory count, updating of inventory reports/records and reconciliation. A complete physical count was conducted in 2022 and 2021; records from prior years were reviewed; one-time cleansing process was initiated and is expected to aid the complete reconciliation of records. However, some records remain unreconciled due to insufficient documentation.
observations.reason: Although a partial/complete physical count was undertaken and a one-time cleansing initiated, reconciliation remains incomplete due to limited availability of supporting documents and records; GSO and Accounting still need to finalize the one-time cleansing and reconcile outstanding items.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant to intensify consistent and regular issuance of demand letters to the accountable officers and employees, both active and inactive, copy furnished the COA Audit Team; in coordination with other responsible departments, and the Local Chief Executive, facilitate exhaustion of all possible remedies to demand liquidation of all cash advances especially those accountable officers/employees still connected with the agency and impose sanctions such as withholding of salaries and filing appropriate charges, if warranted, as prescribed in Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular 1997-002; the concerned Department Heads and Officers/Employees still connected with the agency to immediately liquidate all of their outstanding cash advances by submitting all the required supporting documents related to payments/purchases made therefrom.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were issued to the accountable officers; however, only those active employees responded. The concerned Department Heads and Officers/Employees still connected with the agency were directed to immediately submit their liquidation papers and supporting documents. Some sanctions such as salary withholding were considered but could not be implemented given limitations on allowable deductions to protect minimum net take-home pay. The Accounting Office intensified issuance of demand letters and coordinated with LCE for further actions.
observations.reason: Application of withholding or other sanctions was constrained by regulations limiting allowable payroll deductions which would reduce net take-home pay below prescribed thresholds; limited response/compliance from accountable officers; ongoing collection and verification efforts.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant to: a. substantiate the account recorded as 'For Reconciliation, beginning balances 5/31/2007' under the Trust Fund SL amounting to (P232,970.18), and act accordingly to resolve the negative balances thereof; b. book adjusting entry to reinstate the liability as a consequence of the stale checks: Cash in Bank - LBP (2192-1001-20) P344,663.93 Liability Account P344,663.93; c. ensure regular reconciliation of balances of the Cash in Bank and Sinking Fund accounts between the general ledger/subsidiary ledger (GL/SL) and the cashbook; d. prepare and submit complete and updated Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS); and e. cancel and replace various stale checks which remained not cancelled and replaced, resulting to understatement of P344,663.93 in both Cash in Bank and Accounts Payable accounts.
observations.managementAction: Suggested adjusting entry to reinstate the liability as a consequence of the stale checks was already partially implemented. For stale checks covering the period of CY 2015 and below, the determination of payees was already coordinated with the MTO. Reconciliation efforts between cashbook and GL/SL were undertaken; some constraints remained due to limited records. The Accounting Office continued to prepare and submit BRS and to address stale checks.
observations.reason: Implementation is partially done: suggested entry partially implemented; stale checks dating back to 2015 and prior require further investigation to determine payees; limited availability of records constrains full reconciliation and cancellation/replacement of stale checks.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Budget Officer to consider the use of an automated budgeting system consistent with the accounting system in order to facilitate and synchronize the preparation of the reports and monitoring of funds, with the related reports from the accounting and other departments. Also, ensure reconciliation between related reports of LDF-PPAs prepared by the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Budget Office.
observations.managementAction: There was a request through the Provincial Budget Office to conduct e-Budget Orientation to interested LGUs. The LGU is still waiting for the Provincial Budget Office/DBM XII to implement the Budget Office e-Budget System. Non-reconciliation issues between MPDO and MBO reports were recognized and coordination efforts were underway to harmonize reports.
observations.reason: Limited budget and the LGU is awaiting implementation and orientation schedule for the DBM's e-Budget System; reconciliation issues persist until automated system and schedules are available.

observations.recommendation: 1. The Sangguniang Bayan, in cooperation with the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Budget Officer, to adequately fund the budgetary requirements for the implementation of SWM-related PPAs as programmed in the Plan, including, where practicable, those that have already lapsed since the effectivity thereof in 2015. 2. The Municipal Ecological Waste Management Board, as assisted by the MENRO Chief, as head secretariat thereof, to: (a) review previous SWM PPAs, as programmed, particularly those that were left unimplemented since the effectivity Plan, and devise strategies on how to implement them henceforward, together with the PPAs of the coming years, with the eventual approval of the budget required thereon; and (b) ensure that sufficient monitoring and review procedures over the implementation of SWM PPAs are properly in place, to make certain the effective attainment of the Plan's objectives.
observations.managementAction: Provision of MOOE to mobilize other LGU PPAs that were tagged as "priority" and implementation of revenue generation ordinances to augment financial requirement for identified PPAs under SWM plan were proposed. The MENRO/MEWMB prepared proposals for allocation of budget for PPAs but implementation is subject to availability of funds. Monitoring of SWM PPAs was conducted; not all PPAs implemented due to limited resources.
observations.reason: Limited resources to fund all required PPAs; budget proposals were prepared but implementation depends on availability of funds; monitoring is being done but resource constraints prevented full implementation.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management
direct the following officers to:

The Municipal Accountant:
a) Conduct regular reconciliation of PPE accounts with the records of the GSO;
b) Ensure that the accounting procedures are done in a timely manner through
proper time management and sound internal control in order to achieve a fair
presentation of financial statements;
c) Prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the
local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local
Road Network Ledger Card, respectively;
d) Support the JV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads, working
papers on the distribution of costs for the road components, and working
papers on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost for road
components with no available cost per registry;
e) Keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for
every road network; and
f) Include disclosures of the Local Road Network System in the Notes to
Financial Statements in the aforementioned prescribed format.

The General Services Officer:
a) Conduct a complete annual physical inventory of all properties of the local
government unit as of December 31 of each year, as mandated by the
pertinent regulations;
b) Maintain and monitor Property Cards (PC) in order to have a sound internal
control over the monitoring of the PPEs;
c) Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and,
d) Conduct a physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of
the Road Network System.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit all
pending Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) from January 2021 to the
present, together with the required supporting documents such as bank
statements and debit/credit memos;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and effect adjusting or
correcting journal entries for the noted discrepancies;
c. Ensure regular preparation and submission of the BRS in compliance
with existing rules and regulations to avoid the accumulation of
unreconciled balances;
d. Require the Treasurer’s Office to update all cash books, particularly
those related to PhilHealth and BIR accounts, which were found to be
outdated or not maintained at all; these records must reflect complete,
accurate, and up-to-date transactions to support reliable financial
reporting;
e. Ensure that the duties of the Treasury and Accounting Offices are
properly segregated to prevent conflict of interest and minimize the risk
of error, fraud, or misappropriation; clearly assign the maintenance of
cash books to the proper personnel, and no single office to control both
recording and disbursement functions;
f. Optimize the use of the LBP WeAccess platform for real-time access to
bank statements, and direct the trained staff to regularly generate and
utilize this data to avoid delays in reconciliation and financial monitoring;
and,
g. Direct the Internal Audit Services or any designated oversight unit to
conduct periodic reviews of cash management practices, ensure the
timeliness of BRS submission, and compliance with reconciliation
procedures, and any lapses noted be immediately addressed and
reported.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management:
i. Strictly implement the provisions of COA Circular No. 2022-004 and COA
Circular 2024-006 by adopting the updated capitalization threshold of
P50,000.00, ensuring that tangible items below this amount be properly
classified and recorded as semi-expendable property to ensure
compliance with existing accounting and auditing standards;
ii. Direct the Municipal Accountant to review and reclassify previously
capitalized items that fall below the P50,000.00 threshold, as well as
prepare and make necessary adjustments to correct misstatements in
the affected asset, expense, and equity accounts to fairly present the
financial position of the municipality;
iii. Exert all necessary efforts to trace, verify, and update the Property, Plant,
and Equipment (PPE) records, including conducting a thorough
reconciliation between the records maintained by the Accounting Office
and those of the General Services Office (GSO) to ensure consistency,
accuracy, and completeness of the reported balances; and
iv. Direct the concerned personnel to regularly participate in capacity-
building activities, including formal training and refresher courses on
government accounting policies, asset management, and internal control
procedures, to enhance their competence and ensure proper adherence
to updated rules, thereby strengthening the municipality’s financial
management and accountability systems.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) the Municipal Accountant establish strong internal controls to ensure that

discrepancies do not recur in future years. Regular monitoring of PPE accounts
and timely reconciliation with subsidiary ledgers should be enforced to ensure
compliance with NGAS and IPSAS 1; and
b) the Municipal Mayor spearhead in allocating the necessary resources and
manpower to support the accounting department and GSO in identifying and
addressing the discrepancies. This may include additional personnel, training,
and technical support to properly reconcile and validate PPE balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) effect the necessary adjusting entry:

Account Title Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustment 362,924.40
Construction in Progress – Infrastructure
Assets 362,924.40

b) henceforth, costs related to routine repairs and maintenance, such as labor and
consumables for repairs and other PPE accounts, should not be capitalized but
rather treated as current period expenses; and

c) submit to the Audit Team the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) and supporting
documents reflecting the corrections made.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) the Municipal Accountant:

i. henceforth, reclassify completed projects from CIP to PPE even if labor
cost is not yet received by the accounting department, provided the asset
is already in use;

ii. work closely with the Engineering Department to compile all necessary
documentation and report these completed projects as PPE in the
financial statements, ensuring that CIP is not overstated, and PPE is not
understated; and

iii. establish a periodic review process for all CIP projects to monitor
progress, reclassification, and to ensure that no completed projects are
overlooked or left unreported.

b) the Municipal Engineer ensure that all accomplishment reports or other reports
for completed projects are prepared and submitted to the Municipal Accountant
in a timely manner to allow proper classification and eliminate discrepancies
between the actual status of projects and what is reflected in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) direct the Municipal Accountant to:
i. effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the balance
under Prepaid Insurance account and Prepaid Registration account:
Account Title Account Code Debit Credit
Prepaid Insurance 1-05-01-050 ₱672,855.64
Prepaid Registration 1-05-01-030 36,618.17
Prior Period Adjustment 3-01-01-020 ₱ 709,473.81

ii. consistently observe the provisions of Section 62 and Section 64 of the NGAS
(Volume I) on the treatment of prepayments to achieve fair and reliable
financial reporting.

b) instruct the General Services Officer to:
i. ensure the timely processing of motor vehicle and construction equipment
registration to avoid incurring penalties and ensure compliance with LTO
regulations; and

ii. establish a monitoring system to track the registration status and renewal
dates of all the municipality’s vehicles and construction equipment to prevent
delays in the future.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) direct the Municipal Accountant to:

i. observe the proper account titles in recording transactions involving semi-
expendable property, in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2024-
006, to ensure correct classification and presentation in the financial
statements;

ii. prepare and maintain the Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card (SPLC) as
prescribed under Annex A.2 of COA Circular No. 2022-004, to ensure that all
acquisitions, descriptions, issuances, transfers, impairments, repairs, and
disposals of semi-expendable property are properly recorded and monitored
in the accounting records; and

iii. coordinate with the Municipality’s General Services Office to streamline the
process of recording transactions involving semi-expendable property. This
includes ensuring the proper use of prescribed forms, timely recording of
issuances, and consistent reconciliation of records to enhance accountability
and compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004.

b) instruct the Supply Officer to:

i. prepare, maintain, and regularly update the prescribed inventory records
for Semi-Expendable Property, enumerated in Section 4.7 of COA
Circular No. 2022-004, to ensure monitoring and accountability of issued
items; and

ii. collaborate with the Accounting Office to align procedures on
documentation, issuance, and reconciliation of records for consistent and
compliant property management.

We recommended and Management agreed that:

a) the Municipal Accountant:

i. assign personnel to oversee the proper disposition of dormant accounts and
ensure adherence to the guidelines prescribed by COA;

ii. conduct a comprehensive review and validation of all receivable accounts, with
38
a particular focus on identifying those that have been inactive for ten (10) years
or more, and classify them in accordance with the definitions under COA
Circular No. 2023-008;

iii. initiate immediate actions for the disposition of dormant accounts, including
gathering and securing available supporting documents, tracing debtors or
accountable officers where possible, documenting efforts to locate untraceable
or deceased debtors, and recommending qualified accounts for write-off,
supported by complete documentation;

iv. send confirmation letters to concerned NGAs and LGUs to verify the existence
and status of the receivables;

v. prepare and submit a formal request for write-off of eligible dormant
receivables to the COA, following the detailed procedures and documentary
requirements outlined in COA Circular Nos. 2023-008 and 2016-005;

vi. develop and implement an internal monitoring system to regularly track the
status and aging of receivable accounts. This should include the assignment of
focal personnel responsible for periodic reconciliation, verification, and
follow-up of outstanding accounts to prevent similar issues in the future;

vii. implement routine validation procedures, including annual confirmation of
balances with debtors, documentation of collection attempts and, immediate
investigation of long-outstanding accounts to prevent the accumulation of
dormant and uncollectible receivable accounts;

viii. consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers with
outstanding dormant cash advances and are still employed with the LGU,
otherwise, further administrative actions may be pursued to enforce
compliance; and

ix. disclose all dormant or questionable accounts appropriately in the Notes to
Financial Statements in the absence of timely write-off, in order to preserve
transparency and provide accurate information to users of financial reports.

b) the Municipal Mayor oversee the timely submission to COA of documentary
requirements for accounts recommended for write-off, including sworn
certifications, justification reports, and evidence of exhausted collection efforts.

1. the Municipal Accountant: a. immediately facilitate the preparation and submission of the required BRS pursuant to Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No.96-011 dated October 2, 1996; b. in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, reconcile the discrepancy noted between the subsidiary ledger and cashbook and henceforth ensure the daily reconciliation of records in order to come up with a more reliable/accurate balances of the Cash in Bank accounts as reflected in the accounting, cashbooks and bank records; and c. assign personnel to solely focus on the substantiation of the account recorded as “For Reconciliation, beginning balances 5/31/2007” under the Trust Fund SL amounting to (P232,970.18). 2. the Municipal Treasurer strictly maintain cashbooks of all the Cash in Bank account transactions for all funds of the Agency with AGDBs; and 3. the Municipal Mayor expedite the processing and approval of any future requests for additional personnel in order to ensure the timely preparation of BRS.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant facilitated the preparation and submission of the required BRS pursuant to Section 74 of PD No. 1445; the Municipal Accountant facilitated the preparation and submission of the required BRS by designating one accounting staff member to take charge of the task. AAR Balances between the two reports were already reconciled. Personnel were already assigned to focus on the substantiation of the account; however, due to the limited availability of records, the attempt was ultimately unsuccessful.
observations.reason: However, due to the staff member’s involvement in various LGU committees and her numerous responsibilities within the Accounting Department, she was unable to devote sufficient time to the preparation of the BRS, which ultimately led to its non-submission. Nevertheless, reconciliation efforts were undertaken and personnel were designated to focus on the task; due to limited availability of records the substantiation attempt was unsuccessful.

observations.recommendation: 1. The Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the LDRRM Council, guarantee the best possible use and of the LDRRMF in order to strengthen and improve the LGU's and its constituents' ability to address and/or respond to emergencies, disasters, and calamities. 2. The MDRRMO to: a. henceforth prevent charging expenses that are not in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 10121, NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002, as well as other relevant laws, rules, and regulations governing the use of DRRM Fund; and b. in collaboration with the LDRRM Council, conduct a thorough evaluation of the proposed project before including it into the plan to ascertain the effective allocation of resources and the successful implementation of initiatives that benefit the community.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor coordinated with the LDRRM Council and the MDRRMO conducted situational analyses and other evaluations; procurement reviews and monitoring procedures were undertaken. The MDRRMO indicated that they will ensure projects are aligned with DRRM purposes and that non-compliant purchases will be avoided. A notice of disallowance was warned for non-compliant transactions.
observations.reason: There was adoption of measures to align procurements and PPAs to DRRM objectives and to strengthen evaluation and monitoring; however, some actions remain subject to ongoing evaluation and continued monitoring.

observations.recommendation: 1. The Municipal Mayor consider hiring/assigning additional staff member/s in the Supplies and Property Unit to guarantee full execution of the department’s responsibilities and that the necessary reports are prepared and submitted on time in accordance with the laws, rules, and regulations governing PPE account. 2. The Municipal General Service Officer: a. maintain Property Cards (PCs) on all classes of PPE and henceforth prepare and maintain additional forms, registry, and reports for semi-expendable properties, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2022-004, since issued semi-expendables are already derecognized in the books of accounts; b. incorporate in the Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property (RPCSP) the result of the physical count for the semi-expendables and exclude the foregoing in the RPCPPE; and c. conduct a comprehensive physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment items of the Municipality and subsequently submit to COA a complete RPCPPE, in the prescribed format, not later than January 31 of each year. 3. The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal General Services Office, re-evaluate useful lives of infrastructure assets pursuant to Section 262(e) of the GAM for LGUs, Volume I, and effect the necessary adjustments thereafter.
observations.managementAction: Coordination with HRMO and MBO to consider the hiring of additional personnel was undertaken; the Municipal General Services Officer maintained Property Cards for semi-expendable properties. A complete physical count was conducted for some items; a one-time PPE cleansing process was undertaken and is ongoing to reconcile records. The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the MGS0 for physical inventory count and reconciliation; a partial physical count was done and GSO to process the one-time cleansing to reconcile records.
observations.reason: Hiring of additional personnel is subject to availability of funds; while property cards for semi-expendable properties were prepared and some physical counts were conducted, full reconciliation and completion of the one-time cleansing and submission of complete RPCPPE remain ongoing due to limited records and resource constraints.

observations.recommendation: The Local Chief Executive to conduct thorough situational analysis (research and development) of the Municipality to understand risk information which includes national hazards and vulnerabilities to alleviate poverty, save lives and environmental protection consistent with the purpose of the fund to identify immediately needed activities and possibly realign PPAs to a more responsive one.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive and concerned offices conducted situational analyses and assessments to better identify DRRM priorities and align PPAs accordingly. Efforts were made to realign and prioritize PPAs to ensure better utilization of DRRM funds and improve responsiveness to risk information.
observations.reason: Measures were undertaken to conduct situational analyses and to realign PPAs; implementation is ongoing as part of regular DRRM planning and project evaluation.

observations.recommendation: 1. Require the Collectors to remit their collections intact to the Liquidating Officer so as to avoid the incurrence of shortages. Demand letters should be sent to the concerned collectors as basis for them to pay their unremitted collections. If not acted by them, payroll deduction shall be imposed. 2. Advise also the personnel who were overpaid of their claims to make the necessary refunds (immediately or through salary deduction) of their claims.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were distributed to the concerned personnel and collectors; refunds were made and payments through salary deduction of some collectors was enforced subject to the availability of their net take-home pay. Collections through refund and salary deduction were processed.
observations.reason: Demand letters were issued and refunds as well as salary deductions (subject to availability of net take-home pay) were enforced; collection efforts implemented.

observations.recommendation:
28. Require the City Accountant to prepare Schedule of Fund Transfers to NGOs/POs and LGUs with emphasis columns for status of project implementation and specific period to liquidate the funds.
29. Institute additional measures which the City finds workable to effectively improve its liquidation rate.
observations.managementAction: Comprehensive evaluation conducted and demand letters sent; however, no liquidation or fund utilization reports submitted yet.
observations.reason: No liquidation or fund utilization reports have been submitted yet; requested documentation still pending.

observations.recommendation: 30. Submit the requested justification/explanation on the IAs with revoked licenses to facilitate the appropriate disposition of audit action.
observations.reason: None was submitted for validation.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management direct the Municipal Accountant to implement the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, to submit the BRS together with supporting documents including the bank statements, paid checks, original copies of debit/credit memos within 15 days of the ensuing month as the prescribed period.
Moreover, coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to apply or register the accounts of the Municipality with the respective depository banks’ websites which permit the generation of the statement of accounts electronically at any point in time, when needed, or the WE-Access of the LBP.
observations.managementAction: Applications for We Access are already on process.
observations.reason: Bank Statements were difficult to obtain from the bank. However, applications for We Access are already on process.

observations.recommendation: 1. The Municipal Budget Officer to consider the use of an automated budgeting system consistent with the accounting system in order to facilitate and synchronize the preparation of the reports and monitoring of funds, with the related reports from the accounting and other departments. a. Non-reconciliation between related reports of LDF-PPAs prepared by the Municipal Planning and Development Office and Municipal Budget Office.
observations.managementAction: There was a request through the Provincial Budget Office to DBM XII to implement the DBM's e-Budget System that is currently undergoing revision. Proposal for the allocation of budget for the PPAs was done but subject to the availability of fund.
observations.reason: Limited budget and the LGU is still waiting for the Provincial Budget Office to DBM XII to conduct e-Budget Orientation to interested LGUs like ours but is still pending due to unavailability of schedule. Monitoring of SWM PPAs was conducted. Not all PPAs are implemented due to limited resources.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Request immediately the termination of all Time Deposits held with
the three banks and facilitate their transfer to appropriate current
bank accounts;
b. Require the City Accountant to reclassify at once the Time Deposits
account to Current Account as soon as relevant documents of
termination of TD and transfer to current account were received from
the banks, as follows:
Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Current Account xx
Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Time Deposits xx
c. Require the City Accountant to provide data (financial information,
documents, records, reports) relative to the opening of all time
deposit accounts with the banks, such as details of source of funds
on various PPAs under trust funds, approval of Sangguniang
Panlungsod (SP) and Local Chief Executive (LCE);
d. Strengthen internal control and procedures to include regular reviews
of trust fund placements and adherence to various guidelines to
prevent similar occurrences in the future.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Instruct the Local Finance Committee to determine the income
reasonably projected as collectible for the ensuing year as mandated
by Section 316 of RA 7160 by considering historical figures and
estimates of sources of revenues for the past five (5) years or more;
b. Ensure projected expenditures should be within the anticipated
income;
c. Enforce a stronger collection policy of the Hospital’s receivables from
various agencies to increase cash inflows and evaluate their
collectability accordingly;
d. Instruct the Hospital Director and other accountable officers to
practice restraints and prudence in the disbursement of funds
through the introduction of cost-cutting measures and limiting the
expenditures to essential operating needs;
e. Direct the City Treasurer to strengthen appropriate measures to
control and monitor the cash availability of the Hospital and to refrain
from certifying cash availability to support the incurrence of
obligations and payables in the absence of actual and sufficient cash;
and
f. Consider implementing the procedures and guidelines prescribed
under LBC 111 to evaluate and improve the operations and fiscal
administration of the Hospital, so that financial self-sufficiency and
cash deficit alleviation can be achieved.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Direct Inventory Committee, CAO and CGSO to investigate and clear
all noted variances in accounting and property records anchoring on
the RPCPPE and accordingly submit the results of investigation to the
Audit Team for further assessment;
b. Direct the CAO and CGSO to faithfully execute the preliminary
activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking in accordance with
Executive Order No. 53, s. 2022;
c. Direct the Inventory Committee to faithfully execute the inventory
taking procedures in accordance with Executive Order No. 53, s. 2022
and ensure the correctness and completeness of RPCPPE before
submission;
d. Direct the CAO and CGSO to faithfully execute the reconciliation
procedures of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and
accounting records in accordance with Executive Order No. 53, s.
2022;
e. If the property and accounting records are reconciled:
i. Direct the CAO and CGSO to faithfully execute the disposition
procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs in accordance with
Executive Order No. 53, s. 2022;
ii. Direct the Disposal Committee, CAO and CGSO to proceed with
the disposal of unserviceable properties in accordance with
COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 or the Revised Manual on
the Disposal of Government Properties;
iii. Direct the CAO to derecognize the undepreciated PPE items
with no available records; Otherwise, compute for its
depreciation and accordingly adjust the books.
f. If, after all the procedures have been taken in accordance with
Executive Order No. 53, s. 2022 and the non-reconciliation between
property and accounting records still persists, consider
implementing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA
Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Provide payment details and other relevant specifications of all the
identified terminated projects as of December 31, 2023 and submit to
the Audit Team for assessment;
b. Require the CEO to promptly comply and submit to CAO all required
supporting documents for projects remarked as terminated; and
c. Direct the CAO to effect immediately the necessary adjustments of all
the identified terminated projects to their appropriate accounts upon
receipt of required documents.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Require the CAO to further review and assess the propriety of
classification of identified PPE acquisitions and completions, in
accordance with COA Circular 2015-009, and accordingly draw up
necessary adjusting journal entries;
b. Stop relying on the account codes used in budgetary documents and
integrate the description of chart of accounts as provided by Annex
B of COA Circular 2015-009 in analyzing transactions for recording;
c. If possible, incorporate in the budgeting process already the proper
use of chart of accounts to harmonize the account codes in
budgetary documents and journal entry vouchers.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Direct the CAO to review and reclassify the items which did not meet
the capitalization threshold of P50,000.00 in accordance with COA
Circular 2022-004 and 2024-006;
b. Direct the CGSO to check and update accordingly the forms,
registries and reports required by COA Circular 2022-004 and 2024-
006 pertaining to the noted items; and
c. Moving forward, direct the CAO to effectively incorporate the new
capitalization threshold in accordance with COA Circular 2022-004
and new account titles as provided by Annex B of COA Circular 2024-
006 in the recording process to avoid misclassifying semi-
expendable properties as PPE and vice-versa.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the CAO to:
a. Revise the Notes 3.17 and 9 in accordance with the disclosure
requirements of IPSAS 17 and submit to the Office of the Auditor;
b. Ensure proper compliance with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS
17, specifically, to:
i. Enumerate explicitly the useful lives used for each PPE class;
and
ii. Consider using the pro-forma schedule provided by the Audit
Team in disclosing the PPE balances in the financial statements;
c. Enforce stricter review procedures in checking the accuracy and
appropriateness of the disclosure in the financial statements,
especially, to:
i. Tie up the additions, disposals, transfers, adjustments,
depreciation, etc. as disclosed in the financial statements with
the lapsing schedule; and
ii. Ensure that the roll forward of comparative balances aligns with
prior year disclosures in the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Liquidate the cash advances for the 4th quarter at year-end as
provided in Section 6.2.2.7 of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JMC No.
2015-01 dated January 08, 2015. If there is any cut-off policy for
recording year-end expenses, it should be extended to include the
liquidation of Confidential Funds (CF);
b. Direct the City Accountant's Office to record transactions only when
they are supported with sufficient and appropriate documents, such
liquidation of CF should be supported with the copy of the transmittal
of documents duly acknowledged by the ICFAU before recording the
liquidation; and
c. Moving forward, consider/be mindful of the particular guidelines for
different financial transactions, such as Petty Cash Funds (PCF)
should adhere to COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997,
while Confidential Funds (CF) should follow the COA-DBM-DILG-
GCG-DND JMC No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015., etc.

We recommended and the City Peace and Order Council and its Secretariat agreed to:
a. Revisit and familiarize existing guidelines on the release and use of
Confidential Fund to ensure strict compliance on the required
procedures and accuracy of all pertinent details in the Resolutions;
b. Ensure that the mandatory review and approval process of
documents are duly undertaken to ensure it is free from material
errors and complies with the requirements set forth in the guidelines;
c. Take proactive measures in preparation and communication. Prepare
ahead and notify all members of the CPOC with ample lead time to
prioritize attendance; and
d. Approve the CPOC Resolutions authorizing the use and release of CF
through the required votes of at least 2/3 of the regular members of
the CPOC.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Ensure proposed development projects for the year are well-planned
and procurement-and-implementation-ready by enhancing the
planning process and procurement through a collaborative effort
among the concerned key officials of the local government unit e.g.
Local Development Council, CEO, BAC, CPDO, end-users and others;
b. Implement religiously, as planned, all programmed projects under the
20% LDF during the current year to ensure that its objectives of
desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes will be
achieved;
c. Conduct comprehensive reviews and assessments of all the
unimplemented development projects, taking into consideration past
experiences vis a vis laws, rules, and regulations to pinpoint
challenges and prevent their recurrence, e.g. project duplication,
obsolescence, price escalation of materials, contractor issues, and
other justifiable reasons for unimplementability;
d. Maximize and mobilize existing human resources on the issue of lack
of workforce with the CEO to implement projects, programs, and
activities of offices and officers concerned instead of proposing and
allocating funds for additional manpower but not realized at year-end;
and
e. Moving forward, adhere strictly to the existing relevant laws, rules,
and regulations, approved plans and targets e.g. RA 7160, DBM-DOF-
DILG JMC No. 1., LGU’s targets and accomplishments for the specific
period, SGLG Assessment criteria and other others.

We recommended and Management to:
a. Justify why the foregoing expenditures for grants and donations in the total
actual disbursements of P187.581 million should not be disallowed in the audit.
Otherwise, appropriate audit action will be undertaken.
b. Refrain from charging grants and donations expenditures to the 20%
Development Fund, and if there are any balances, reallocate the funds for the
Scholarship Grants/Expenses – Alay sa Kabataang Generals Lahat ng Alaga at
Talino (AKLAT), Donations – Emergency Assistance Program for Indigent
People (EAPIP), and Donations – Auxiliaries and Medical Program for
Individuals and Needy Generals (AMPING) to their appropriate budget
categories which should be funded under education and social
services/healthcare budgets, respectively.
c. Identify development projects during the initial planning stage which are aligned
with the guidelines as provided by laws, rules and regulations, such as RA 7160
and DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 e.g. socio-economic
infrastructure projects like roads, bridges, and public facilities.
d. Conduct a comprehensive review of the existing local development plans to
ensure all projects align with the criteria for the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended and Management thru Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) agreed to:
a. Provide justification on the noted delays in particular procurement
stages; and
b. Strictly comply with the required procurement timelines to ensure
efficient and timely acquisition of goods necessary for government
operation and delivery of public service.

We recommended and Management thru Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) agreed to:
a. Provide justification on why the two-stage competitive bidding was
not employed in the procurement of Traffic Signalization and CCTV
Synchronization Project considering the project’s complexity in
nature and materiality of the amount; and
b. For complex projects, engage on experts/professional with sufficient
technical expertise and employ a two-stage competitive bidding to
provide a well-defined, precise set of technical specifications,
accurate cost estimation, optimal contractual terms and enhanced
project reliability.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Direct the City Accountant’s Office, in coordination with the City
Human Resource Management and Development Office, to fully
comply with the disclosure requirements for related party
transactions, especially those concerning the key management
personnel and their close family members, in adherence to Par. 34 of
IPSAS 20; and
b. Require the City Accountant’s Office to prepare and maintain a
monitoring schedule or report regarding the remuneration and
compensation received of the key management personnel and their
close family members in aid of the disclosure requirements of Par. 34
of IPSAS 20.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the City Accountant’s Office to:
a. Restate the prior year balances of the affected accounts shown as
comparative figures for the current year financial statements in
compliance with the requirements under Par. 47 of IPSAS 3;
b. Fully comply with the disclosure requirements for prior period
adjustments in adherence to Par. 54 of IPSAS 3; and
c. Consider using the pro-forma disclosures provided by the Audit Team
in presenting the retrospective restatement of prior period errors in
the financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a. Provide justification as to the actions made in closing the dormant
Due to LGUs account despite lack of basis;
b. Direct the CAO to revert the closing journal entries made in the Due

to LGUs account; and
c. Moving forward, require the CAO to only close the nominal accounts
in the books, unless there is an underlying basis to close real
accounts.

The Municipal Accountant:
a) Conduct regular reconciliation of PPE accounts with the records of the GSO;
b) After the count, trace any discrepancy with the RPCPPE and perform proper
derecognition and disposition procedures, together with other concerned
personnel, for non-existing/missing PPE;
c) Ensure that the accounting procedures are done in a timely manner through
proper time management and sound internal control in order to achieve a fair
presentation of financial statements;
d) Prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the local
road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road
Network Ledger Card, respectively;
e) Support the JV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads, working
papers on the distribution of costs for the road components, and on the
determination of the depreciated replacement cost for road components with no
available cost per registry;
f) Keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every
road network; and
g) Include disclosures of the Local Road Network System in the Notes to Financial
Statements in the aforementioned prescribed format.

The General Services Officer:
a) Conduct a complete annual physical inventory of all properties of the local
government unit as of December 31 of each year, as mandated by the pertinent
regulations;
b) Maintain and monitor Property Cards (PC) in order to have sound internal
control over the monitoring of the PPEs;
c) Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its
components; and
d) Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map.

The Inventory Committee:
a) Conduct a complete physical count and prepare the duly accomplished RPCPPE
including the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We also recommended that the management start the formation of the Inventory
Committee in order to facilitate the conduct of the “One Time Cleansing” operation
of the Municipality in accordance with COA Circular 2020-0062.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management
direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Coordinate with the source agency pertaining to the reversion of the
unexpended balance and/or make a representation for any possible
reprogramming of the said balances; and

b. Ensure that all the transactions were recorded, specifically pertaining to the
completion of the projects and the recognition of the corresponding assets.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management
direct the following:

Municipal Accountant:
Prepare journal entry/ies to transfer the completed PPE from the CIP account to the
corresponding PPE accounts as soon as it is completed and available for use in
order to ensure the proper recognition and recording of completed projects as
assets.

General Services Officer:
Conduct an annual physical inventory of all supplies and properties of the local
government unit as of December 31 of each year, as mandated by the pertinent
regulations.

We also recommended that the management start to direct the formation of the
Inventory Committee in order to facilitate the conduct of the “One Time Cleansing”
operation of the Municipality in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the management direct the
Municipal Accountant to:

a. Properly check and reconcile the encoded entries, specifically the intra-agency
accounts that are reciprocal in nature;
b. Book the unaccounted transactions and ensure that all transactions have been
duly recorded and the corresponding accounts have been duly established;
c. Reclassify to their proper accounts those misposted or misclassified in the
reciprocal accounts; and
d. Exert effort in determining the nature of the transaction of those beginning
balances for CY 2016 in order to facilitate proper classification and/or correcting
entries, if necessary.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management
direct the following officers to:

The Municipal Accountant:
a) Recompute, and verify the amount and details of the Special Trust Fund Account
and the Continuing Appropriation pertaining to the Unexpended Balances due to
the STF and make the necessary adjustments and corrections to the account title
and transfer of the appropriate amount to the STF in accordance with the
provisions of RA 10121 and COA Circular 2012-002; and,
b) Verify the amount and details of the DRRMF and disclose the accurate data in the
Notes to the Financial Statement in accordance with the format prescribed in COA
Circular 2012-002;
The Municipal DRRM Officer:
a) Ensure that the PPAs charged from the previous year’s (PY) unexpended
balances lodged in the Special Trust Fund are duly indicated in the Annual
Investment Plan prior to the release and utilization thereof; and,
b) Ensure that the preparation of the LDRRMFIP conforms with the provisions of COA
Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and includes the projects and
activities to be sourced from the previous year’s unexpended balances;
The MPDC:
a) Coordinate with the MDRRMO and prepare the necessary revised AIPs in
accordance with the provisions stated in COA Circular 2012-002 and RA 10121.

In line with the foregoing, we recommended to the management the following
courses of actions:

a) Reintegrate or update the composition of the MIWASH Council in order to
properly determine the persons in-charge with the adopted programs regarding the
Municipal Integrated Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene (MIWASH);
b) Reevaluate and reassess the MW4SP-iWaSH plan as adopted by Resolution No.
2020-250 in order to check the viability of the indicated targets therein,
specifically include step-by-step procedures on how to achieve such targets for a
more instructive and clear plan towards its objectives;
c) Propose and enact a Septage and Sewage Management Ordinance (SSMO) of
the Municipality;
d) Ensure full compliance with the policy and guidelines stipulated in DILG
Memorandum Circular 2019-62 dated April 22, 2019, and its adherence with the
provisions of the Philippine Clean Water Act of 2004; and
e) Prioritize projects that can address pollution prevention, control, and abatement
in the water resources.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) the Municipal Mayor consider hiring/assigning additional staff member/s in the
Supplies and Property Unit to guarantee full execution of the department’s
responsibilities and that the necessary reports are prepared and submitted on
time in accordance with the laws, rules, and regulations governing PPE accounts;
b) the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the technical experts employed
by the LGU, shall re-evaluate the useful life of the infrastructure assets pursuant
to Section 262(e) of the GAM for LGUs, Volume I, and effect the result thereafter; and
c) the Municipal General Service Officer:
i. maintain Property Cards (PCs) on all classes of PPE and henceforth prepare
and maintain additional forms, registry, and reports for semi-expendable
properties, as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2022-004, since issued semi-
expendables are already derecognized in the books of accounts;
ii. incorporate in the Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable
Property (RPCSP) the result of the physical count for the semi-expendables
and exclude the foregoing in the RPCPPE; and
iii. conduct a comprehensive physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment
items of the Municipality and subsequently submit to COA a complete
RPCPPE, in the prescribed format, not later than January 31 of each year

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) the Municipal Mayor:
i. ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002
on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund
transfers to implementing agencies, to prevent the accumulation of
unsettled balances;
ii. in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, make a formal request
with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of dormant unliquidated
cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-008;
iii. direct the concerned Officers and Employees still connected with the
agency to immediately submit their liquidation papers to settle their
unliquidated cash advances; and
iv. consider applying the appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those
erring accountable officers and be reminded that the Auditor may order
the suspension of their salaries, as cited in Section 9.2 and 9.3 of COA
Circular No. 2012-004.
b) the Municipal Accountant:
i. consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers with
outstanding cash advances and are still employed with the LGU to enforce
settlement thereof; and
ii. refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee
with existing unliquidated cash advance. However, if it is necessary to
grant cash advances for special activities, Management must grant the
same to the designated Special Disbursing Officer.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) the Municipal Accountant:
i. immediately facilitate the preparation and submission of the required BRS
pursuant to Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No.96-011 dated
October 2, 1996;
ii. in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, reconcile the discrepancy noted
between the subsidiary ledger and cashbook and henceforth ensure the daily
reconciliation of records in order to come up with a more reliable/accurate
balances of the Cash in Bank accounts as reflected in the accounting,
cashbooks and bank records; and
iii. assign personnel to solely focus on the substantiation of the account recorded
as “For Reconciliation, beginning balances 5/31/2007” under the Trust Fund
SL amounting to (P232,970.18).
b) the Municipal Treasurer strictly maintain cashbooks of all the Cash in Bank
account transactions for all funds of the Agency with AGDBs; and
c) the Municipal Mayor expedite the processing and approval of any future requests
for additional personnel in order to ensure the timely preparation of BRS.

With the above observation, we recommended and the Management agreed
that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO): (a)
henceforth prevent charging expenses that are not in accordance with the provisions
of RA No. 10121, NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1
and COA Circular No. 2012-002, as well as other relevant laws, rules, and
regulations governing the use of DRRM Fund, otherwise a corresponding Notice of
Disallowance will be issued; and (b) in collaboration with the LDRRM Council,
conduct a thorough evaluation of the proposed project before including it into the
plan to ascertain the effective allocation of resources and the successful
implementation of initiatives that benefit the community.

1. Require the City Accountant to reclassify the savings account.

6. Enforce stronger collection policy of the Hospital's receivables from various agencies to increase cash inflows and evaluate accordingly their collectability.
7. Instruct the Hospital Director and other accountable officers to practice restraints and prudence in the disbursement of funds through introduction of cost-cutting measures and limiting the expenditures to essential operating needs;
8. Direct the City Treasurer to strengthen appropriate measures to control and monitor the cash availability of Hospital and to refrain from certifying cash availability to support the incurrence of obligations and payables in the absence of actual and sufficient cash; and

observations.managementAction:
There are still AOs who have yet to settle their accounts.
A decrease in receivable was noted. However, the material amount remained uncollected. Accordingly, the hospital accountant has already implemented monitoring of MOA compliance from various funding agencies. Furthermore, they transmit statement of account to the funding agencies on a regular basis. The Hospital acknowledged the audit recommendations and commented that they are still diligently convening multiple meetings to strategize and implement measures aimed at bolstering cash flow. They hired additional job orders to establish an accounts receivable team in the hospital. The Hospital asserted that they have improved the collection from PhilHealth as per their records. The CTO has given assurance that no processing of claims from Hospital were undertaken unless there is sufficient cash available. Also, they have performed periodic reconciliations with the CAO to ensure that their records match. The Hospital was not able to limit their expenditures, especially in hiring additional medical practitioners, as this is required for the Level 2 Accreditation.
observations.reason: A decrease in receivable was noted. However, the material amount remained uncollected. The Hospital has continued to experience negative cash flow; it realized total revenue of P223.612 million while expensed reached P273.404 million, resulting in a negative cash flow of P49.793 million. Previous recommendations were not implemented fully.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the management direct the following officers to:
The Municipal Accountant:
a.Conduct regular reconciliation of PPE accounts with the records of the GSO;
b.After the count, trace any discrepancy with the RPCPPE and perform proper derecognition and disposition procedures, together with other concerned personnel, for non-existing/missing PPE; and
c.Ensure that the accounting procedures are done in a timely manner through proper time management and sound internal control in order to achieve a fair presentation of financial statements.
The General Services Officer:
a.Conduct annual physical inventory of all supplies and properties of the local government unit as of December 31 of each year, as mandated by the pertinent regulations; and
b.Maintain and monitor Property Cards (PC) in order to have sound internal control over the monitoring of the PPE.
We also recommended that the management start the formation of the Inventory Committee in order to facilitate the conduct of the “One Time Cleansing” operation of the Municipality in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the management direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer the following courses of actions:
a.Direct the Accountable officers to remit and report their collections daily, and where collections are minimal and daily deposit thereof becomes costly and impractical, the AOs shall remit their collections at least once a week, or as soon as the collections reach P10,000.00.
b.Ensure that the collections have been recognized on the month of collection and not on the month that it was remitted in order to properly establish the accuracy and timeliness of the figures presented in the submitted reports; and
c.Strictly monitor the accounts and balances of each Accountable Officers through strict submission of the RCDs on a daily basis, or as soon as practicable, to the Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating officer to whom the cash collected should be turned over.

Management’s Comment
According to Provincial Treasurer in a written reply, as well as, during the exit
conference, the back pay certificate of indebtedness had already been adjusted in the cash
position report starting March 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Accounting Department agreed to perform
necessary adjustment/s on the affected asset and liability accounts in the financial
statements as a result of misapplication of recognition criteria and henceforth,
apply the existing policies and standards in the recognition of liability and
construction in progress accounts to all its capital expenditure projects.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed on the following to
enhance utilization and implementation of the 20% Local Development Fund:
a) The PPDO, in coordination with PEO, to develop specific strategies to effectively
implement PPAs, both for current and continuing appropriations. Check
unimplemented PPAs under 20% LDF, determine the causes of non-
implementation and formulate relevant actions to address such.
b) The Provincial Development Council, with the PPDO as secretariat, to ensure
inclusion of all funded local development projects in the approved annual or
supplemental investment programs for a timely, effective and efficient
implementation.
c) The Implementing Departments to ensure that program of works and other
necessary procurement documents be prepared and submitted to PPDO at a
reasonable time in order to hasten the implementation of PPAs during the
planned schedule. Furthermore, The Implementing Departments to also check if
there is a need to recalibrate its manpower accordingly.
d) The Implementing Departments to be responsive and prompt as to requests from
PPDO regarding the progress of their projects in order to eventually improve
the monitoring of PPAs. The PPDO to correspond proactively with regard to the
results of monitoring.

In view of the aforementioned deficiencies in the implementation and
reporting of the PSP Fund, the following recommendations were given which the
management agreed on:
a) The Office of the Provincial Agriculturist (OPAG) as the implementing
department and the Provincial Technical Committee on Palay Support Program
(PTCPSP) created by virtue of IRR-PSP, to formulate specific strategies that will
analyze and determine reasonable stock levels vis-à-vis available PSP fund and
considering the limitations set forth in the existing agreements, laws rules and
regulations.
b) The PTCPSP, in coordination with the OPAG, to monitor and evaluate the
status of PSP implementation along with challenges encountered on a periodic
basis, such as the keeping of massive inventories which is over and above the
needs of various offices and departments, some mishandling thereof in the
storage facilities, weak controls in the procurement, drying and milling operations,
among others. Henceforth, the PTCPSP to develop relevant measures and adopt
effective strategies such as strengthening partnership with the component LGUs
as well as the Department of Agriculture through the National Food Authority
for a more effective and efficient implementation of the PSP.
c) The OPAG to strengthen its close supervision in the management of PSP
inventories and develop relevant controls in ensuring the security, safekeeping
and complete reporting of inventories in the storage facilities such as providing
specific identification of each inventory, proper packaging, piling and
arrangement, periodic monitoring and reporting. Henceforth, ensure that
monthly inventory reports and related supporting documents are submitted to
the concerned offices within the prescribed period as required in existing laws,
rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Provincial Governor agreed to direct the
Provincial Treasurer to identify all dormant depository accounts in order to secure
an authority for closure from the Sangguniang Panlalawigan relative therewith.
Henceforth, the Provincial Treasurer agreed to monitor regularly the cash-in-bank
balances and transactions to determine non-moving bank accounts and to identify
if the purpose had already been fulfilled in order to immediately address the
dormancy status and perform proper disposition during the year.

In view of the foregoing observations, we reiterated and management agreed
on the following recommendations to address the deficiencies which were already
noted in the prior year’s audit:
a) The Provincial Accounting Department to monitor and analyze the actual petty
operating expenses incurred by each office on a periodic basis in order to
authorize/grant the reasonable amount of petty cash at the beginning of the year
and properly address the problems encountered related thereto. Conduct
relevant orientation or re-orientation to designated custodians in order to ensure
compliance with the required procedures in the disbursements, recording, and
monitoring of their cash advances for petty operating expenses.
b) The duly designated petty cash custodians to observe all the policies and
procedures prescribed in the accounting for petty cash fund as provided under
the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs or any other existing
rules and regulations issued relative therewith through the supervision of the
Provincial Accounting Department.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed on the following to
enhance utilization and implementation of the 20% Local Development Fund:
a) The PPDO, in coordination with PEO, to develop specific strategies to effectively
implement PPAs, both for current and continuing appropriations. Check
unimplemented PPAs under 20% LDF, determine the causes of non-
implementation and formulate relevant actions to address such.
b) The Provincial Development Council, with the PPDO as secretariat, to ensure
inclusion of all funded local development projects in the approved annual or
supplemental investment programs for a timely, effective and efficient
implementation.
c) The Implementing Departments to ensure that program of works and other
necessary procurement documents be prepared and submitted to PPDO at a
reasonable time in order to hasten the implementation of PPAs during the
planned schedule. Furthermore, The Implementing Departments to also check if
there is a need to recalibrate its manpower accordingly.
d) The Implementing Departments to be responsive and prompt as to requests from
PPDO regarding the progress of their projects in order to eventually improve
the monitoring of PPAs. The PPDO to correspond proactively with regard to the
results of monitoring.

Therefore, we recommended and the management agreed on the following
recommendations:
a) The implementing departments headed by the Provincial Governor through the
DRRM Officer, to follow the schedule of implementation of pre-disaster PPAs as
embodied in the approved DRRM Investment Plans. In coordination with the
Engineering Department, evaluate the causes of delays in the implementation of
pre-disaster projects in order to provide immediate resolution therein and lessen
or eliminate the vulnerabilities of the community in a timely manner.
b) The PDRRM Council through the DRRM Officer, in coordination with the
Provincial Accounting Department, to properly identify the remaining balances
of DRRM Fund coming from various sources, as a result of fully completed PPAs
and consider its re-appropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs which remained
unfunded. Also, evaluate the status of those unimplemented PPAs if the same is
still to be pursued, otherwise consider its realignment or reversion to other
necessary DRRM PPAs.
c) The Provincial Governor, through the PDRRM Officer, to coordinate with the
implementing department/s in order to facilitate immediate implementation of
programs and projects which was funded through donations.
d) The PDRRM Council, headed by the Provincial Governor, to prepare LDRRMF
Investment Plan for transfers to Special Trust Fund annually. In the case of fund
sources coming from various years’ STF, ensure that the LDRRMF investment
plan is prepared separately for each year in order to properly monitor the
balances and its implementation.
e) The PDRRM Council, headed by the Provincial Governor to ensure specific
project identification upon formulation of its annual LDRRMF investment plan
in close coordination with the Local DRRM Councils (LDRRMCs) for a more
timely, effective and integrated implementation of pre-disaster projects.

We recommended that Management:
• Strictly conduct a complete physical count of all PPE owned by the Province of
Cotabato and strengthen the internal control policy related thereto. Direct the
assigned personnel to expedite the reconciliation process and devote much time
in finishing the Report on the Physical Count of the Property, Plant, and
Equipment (RPCPPE). Keep in mind that the report shall be made annually and
shall be submitted to the auditor concerned no later than January 31st of each
year.
• Reorganize the inventory committee to further enhance the conduct of
inventory taking.
• If warranted, cause the implementation of COA Circular 2020-006 to achieve
fairness in the presentation of the financial position, with reliable and useful
information in decision-making and accountability for PPE.

We recommended the following courses of action to the Management:
• The concerned officials/personnel, particularly the Provincial Accountant, the
Provincial Engineer, the Provincial General Services Officer, and the Inventory
Committee shall strictly perform their duties and responsibilities in the
accounting and reporting of Local Road Networks as mandated under the COA
Circular 2015-008.
• The Provincial Engineering office must prioritize and expedite the assessment
and cost segregation of the road networks' components to provide the necessary
information needed by the Provincial Accountant and Provincial General
Services Officer to maintain the road network account properly.
• The Provincial General Services Office should support and advise the Inventory
Committee to strictly conduct a complete and detailed physical inventory of local
roads owned by the Province; submit the RPCLRN on or before January 31 of
each year; and maintain Property Cards and Local Road Inventory Map for all
roads to be use as a basis for the reconciliation of PPE accounts with accounting
records.

We recommended that Management:
• As a control procedure, require the liquidating officer's approval in the release
of a new set of blank official receipts to the collecting officers.
• Provide capacity building for field collectors in the applicable rules and
regulations, including the correct preparation of necessary reports and use of
official receipts. This helps improve the speed of the reconciliation process
since data are well presented.
• Establish a routine for monitoring and reconciling collections and accountability
forms, as doing so daily is impractical.
• Wherever possible, and with the approval of the Provincial Treasurer,
implement the provisions of Local Treasury Operations Manual section 62 ��b
D on the deposit of field collections. Direct deposit of collections into the
province's account reduces cash accumulation and risk of loss. The deposit
slips will then serve as a supporting document for reconciliation.
• Update the fidelity bond on the average collections that are in the custody of
the collecting officers. This ensures that collections on hand do not exceed the
coverage of fidelity bond.

We recommend that Management:
• Reorganize the PSWMB to effectively discharge the functions and
responsibilities as prescribed in Republic Act 9003.
• The PSWMB should urge and assist the concerned municipalities that do not
have an approved solid waste management plan. Require its strict compliance
for the formulation of the Provincial Solid Waste Management Plan as prescribed
in Section 16 of the act and submit the same to the National Solid Waste
Management Commission for approval.

We recommended that Management:
• Ascertain that fund transferred to the PLGU are effectively used. Assess and
monitor implementation to ensure that it adheres to the criteria agreed upon by
both parties in order to maximize utilization.
• Plan the PPAs well and anticipate any obstacles that may arise prior to starting
their implementation. Conduct monthly meetings to examine the progress of
trust-funded projects in order to immediately resolve any identified issues.
• Following Item 12 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, evaluate the
performance of the contractors using the NEDA-Approved CPES guidelines.
It should be done during the construction and upon completion of each
government project. The implementation of which may be undertaken by
tapping the: (a) In-house CPES evaluators; (b) Third-Party Evaluators; or (c)
Combination of In-house or Third-Party Evaluators.

We recommended that Management:
• Assess the capacity of the devolved hospitals to store supplies. If warranted,
distribute the supplies to the hospitals for their consumption and to decongest
the IPHO warehouse and avoid spoilage.
• Continue coordination with partner offices or agencies to adequately address
areas of concern in the project implementation. Also, fast track the compliance
of necessary permits to be able to begin and complete the construction of a
warehouse in 2022.
• Improve the planning phase by consulting the beneficiaries for problem areas to
develop a responsive solution. Additionally, when crafting PPAs, seek
professional assistance in highly technical areas to develop a holistic plan,
resulting in a more effective and efficient output.
• Prioritize improving the electrical connections of the devolved hospitals to make
the HVAC facilities operational.
• Conduct a proper inspection to determine why some equipment is currently
underutilized and develop a plan to help hospitals address the concerns.
• Instruct the person in charge of updating the BGP-related reports and adhere to
the provisions of LBC 124, Section 4.
• The Provincial Accountant should revert the unused balance of BGP to the
Bureau of Treasury and make the necessary accounting entry. Debit the
Government Equity account and credit the corresponding cash account.

We recommended that Management:
• Direct the concerned offices to qualify PPAs that can be charged against the QRF
and ensure that they are urgent and responsive to the current needs of the
recipient, in order to normalize the situation as quickly as possible and to avoid
stock piling of the related goods.
• If necessary, purchase goods and/or services (charged against the QRF) in
sufficient quantities only to span the time required to purchase new inventories
using the General Fund, Trust Fund, or other available sources, so that the QRF
is not depleted in response to a single circumstance. Observe the application of
the reorder point (ROP) in stockpiling.
• Incorporate the COVID 19 response in the PPAs sourced from the regular agency
fund, given that it may still not end in the near future.

We recommended that the management strictly adhere to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 particularly on the inclusion of loan repayments in order not to sacrifice other priority programs and projects. Moreover, fast track the implementation of the PPAs so that the ultimate beneficiaries could enjoy the improved social, environment and economic condition once the projects are completed. Furthermore, evaluate whether the unimplemented projects from current and prior years’ appropriations are still implementable, or if there is a need to revert funds to be used as new source of appropriations for more priority development projects.

observations.recommendation:
1) The Provincial Governor, through the Provincial Treasurer, to follow up the authority for closure to be issued by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for the requested authority to effect closure of dormant accounts. Henceforth, the Provincial Treasurer to monitor regularly the cash in bank balances and transactions to determine non-moving bank accounts or the purpose had already been satisfied in order to immediately address the dormancy status and perform proper disposition thereof.
2) Require the Provincial Accountant and the Provincial Treasurer to reconcile their respective records pertaining to balances of cash in bank accounts. The Accounting Department to prepare monthly bank reconciliation statement to the newly-opened depository account.
3) The Provincial Governor to facilitate endorsement to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan the letter-request submitted by the Provincial Treasurer which identifies all dormant depository accounts in order to secure an authority for closure relative therewith. Henceforth, the Provincial Treasurer to monitor regularly the cash in bank balances and transactions to determine non-moving bank accounts or the purpose had already been satisfied in order to immediately address the dormancy status and perform proper disposition thereof.
4) Require the Accounting Department to thoroughly review the accounts payable whether the claims are valid and duly supported with complete documentation before recording as such in the books of accounts at year-end. In coordination with the responsible departments, perform periodic monitoring of the recorded accounts payable and ensure immediate settlement thereof within a reasonable period.
